Level_1_Needs_work: No clear domain boundaries; god classes; OrderService API unclear or missing.
Level_2_Getting_there: Classes exist but responsibilities overlap; extending fields risks breaking callers.
Level_3_Acceptable: Clear Product, Customer, Order, OrderItem split; coherent OrderService; stable callers.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus exemplary separation and a credible story for adding catalogue fields without rewriting checkout.

Level_1_Needs_work: Line totals or grand total wrong/missing; quantities unchecked; summary disagrees with internals.
Level_2_Getting_there: Mostly correct totals with occasional inconsistencies or weak validation.
Level_3_Acceptable: Subtotals and grand total match stated rules; quantities validated; summary matches internal computation.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus strong edge-case handling (empty order, multiples) or explicit fail-fast with clear messages.

Level_1_Needs_work: No meaningful tests and/or mvn test fails on submission.
Level_2_Getting_there: Sparse tests; gaps on non-trivial arithmetic or collection behaviour.
Level_3_Acceptable: Tests cover totals and non-trivial cases; mvn test green; regressions would be caught.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus tests that would catch subtle collection/total regressions and document intent.

Level_1_Needs_work: Hard to read; inconsistent style; public fields; dead experimental paths in main.
Level_2_Getting_there: Readable in places; inconsistent encapsulation or naming.
Level_3_Acceptable: Readable, consistent style; sensible encapsulation; clean demo entry point.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus professional structure, naming, and maintainability across packages.

Level_2_Getting_there: Minimal notes; hard to reproduce the demo.
Level_3_Acceptable: Setup/run instructions; comments where rules are non-obvious; optional diagram.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus a precise diagram or short note tying design choices to future change.
